meta.name: Brief-COPE Coping Inventory
meta.description: The Brief-COPE is a 28-item self-report scale that measures 14 distinct coping strategies people use in response to stressful life events. It is a shortened version of the full COPE Inventory and is widely used in health psychology and stress research.
meta.author: Carver (1997)
meta.reference: Carver CS. You want to measure coping but your protocol is too long: consider the Brief COPE. Int J Behav Med. 1997;4(1):92-100.
meta.instruction: The following items ask what you have been doing to cope with a stressful event. Each item says something about a particular way of coping. Please indicate the extent to which you have been doing what the item says.

commonChoices.label: I haven't been doing this at all
I've been doing this a little bit
I've been doing this a medium amount
I've been doing this a lot

questions.text: I've been turning to work or other activities to take my mind off things.
I've been concentrating my efforts on doing something about the situation I'm in.
I've been saying to myself "this isn't real."
I've been using alcohol or other drugs to make myself feel better.
I've been getting emotional support from others.
I've been giving up trying to deal with it.
I've been taking action to try to make the situation better.
I've been refusing to believe that it has happened.
I've been saying things to let my unpleasant feelings escape.
I've been getting help and advice from other people.
I've been using alcohol or other drugs to help me get through it.
I've been trying to see it in a different light, to make it seem more positive.
I've been criticizing myself.
I've been trying to come up with a strategy about what to do.
I've been getting comfort and understanding from someone.
I've been giving up the attempt to cope.
I've been looking for something good in what is happening.
I've been making jokes about it.
I've been doing something to think about it less, such as going to movies, watching TV, reading, daydreaming, sleeping, or shopping.
I've been accepting the reality of the fact that it has happened.
I've been expressing my negative feelings.
I've been trying to find comfort in my religion or spiritual beliefs.
I've been trying to get advice or help from other people about what to do.
I've been learning to live with it.
I've been thinking hard about what steps to take.
I've been blaming myself for things that happened.
I've been praying or meditating.
I've been making fun of the situation.

ranges.label: Low Coping Engagement
ranges.description: Your total Brief-COPE score indicates relatively low engagement with coping strategies overall. This may suggest that you are not actively utilizing available coping resources when facing stressful situations, or that current stressors may feel overwhelming and difficult to address.
suggestions: Consider exploring a broader range of coping strategies, as having multiple tools available helps you respond flexibly to different types of stressors
Even small coping efforts such as talking to a friend, taking a short walk, or writing down your thoughts can make a meaningful difference in managing stress
Reflect on whether low coping engagement may be related to feelings of helplessness or avoidance, and consider speaking with a counselor if stress feels unmanageable

ranges.label: Moderate Coping Engagement
ranges.description: Your Brief-COPE score reflects a moderate level of coping engagement, indicating that you are utilizing some coping strategies when facing stress. The quality of your coping depends on which specific strategies you rely on most, so reviewing your subscale profile is important for understanding your coping pattern.
suggestions: Examine your subscale scores to identify which coping strategies you use most frequently and whether they tend to be adaptive or maladaptive approaches
Consider whether your coping repertoire includes sufficient adaptive strategies such as active coping, planning, positive reframing, and seeking social support
Try to increase your use of problem-focused coping strategies when facing controllable stressors, and emotion-focused strategies for situations beyond your control
If you notice high scores on substance use, denial, or behavioral disengagement subscales, consider seeking professional guidance to develop healthier alternatives
Building a diverse coping toolkit that balances problem-solving, emotional processing, and social support tends to produce the best outcomes

ranges.label: High Coping Engagement
ranges.description: Your Brief-COPE score indicates high engagement with coping strategies, suggesting you actively employ multiple approaches when dealing with stress. While active coping is generally beneficial, it is important to examine your subscale breakdown to ensure you rely primarily on adaptive rather than maladaptive strategies.
suggestions: Review your subscale scores carefully to ensure your coping profile emphasizes adaptive strategies such as active coping, planning, positive reframing, and social support
High scores on substance use, behavioral disengagement, denial, or self-blame subscales may indicate maladaptive coping patterns that warrant professional consultation
Consider whether your high coping engagement might reflect excessive effort or hypervigilance in response to stress, which could itself become a source of exhaustion
Maintain the adaptive strategies that work well for you while gradually reducing reliance on any avoidant or self-destructive coping methods
